title: Assisting with a Lumbar Puncture
subtitle: The nurse assists with a lumbar puncture safely — verifying consent, curling the patient into the fetal position, keeping them still and sterile, labeling CSF tubes, and lying the patient flat afterward to prevent a spinal headache.

patient.name: Lumbar Puncture
patient.admitDate: The nurse assists the provider with a lumbar puncture and provides post-procedure care.

nursesNote: The nurse assists the provider with a lumbar puncture, which collects cerebrospinal fluid, measures pressure, or delivers medication; the needle is placed below the end of the spinal cord (around L3-L4/L4-L5) to avoid cord injury. After confirming informed consent and a time-out, the nurse has the patient empty the bladder and positions them side-lying with the knees drawn to the chest (fetal position) or sitting and leaning forward over a table, which widens the spaces between the vertebrae. A sterile field is maintained to prevent meningitis, and the nurse helps the patient stay still during insertion. The CSF tubes are labeled in order of collection and sent promptly. Afterward the patient lies flat for the ordered time and increases fluids to help replace CSF and prevent a post-LP (spinal) headache, which is positional and worse when sitting or standing. The nurse monitors neurologic status and leg movement/sensation, checks the site for CSF leakage, and reports a severe headache, numbness or tingling, fever, or drainage.
providerOrders: Verify informed consent and have the patient empty the bladder
Position the patient side-lying with knees to the chest (or sitting, leaning forward)
Maintain a sterile field and help the patient hold still
Label CSF tubes in order of collection and send them promptly
Keep the patient flat per order, encourage fluids, and report a severe headache

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about a lumbar puncture?

opts.text: A lumbar puncture collects cerebrospinal fluid for testing
The needle is inserted below the end of the spinal cord
The patient curls into a fetal position to widen the spaces
The bladder should be full during the procedure
Sterile technique is maintained throughout
The patient may move freely while the needle is inserted
Curling forward opens the spaces between the vertebrae
Inserting below the spinal cord avoids cord injury
Lying flat afterward helps prevent a spinal headache
CSF leakage causes the classic post-LP headache
Increasing fluids helps replace CSF and ease headache
A post-LP headache is worse lying down than standing

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order assisting with a lumbar puncture

opts.text: Verify consent and have the patient empty the bladder
Position the patient side-lying with knees to the chest
Help the patient hold still during needle insertion
Label the CSF tubes in order and send them
Position the patient flat and encourage fluids
Verify consent and empty the bladder
Position side-lying with knees to the chest
Maintain a sterile field during insertion
Help the patient hold still as the needle is placed
Position the patient flat afterward
Encourage increased fluids
Monitor for a post-LP headache
Assess the site for CSF leakage

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Hours after the LP the patient reports a severe headache, worse when sitting up
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the headache and neurologic status
Have the patient lie flat
Encourage increased fluids (caffeine per order)
Notify the provider; anticipate analgesia or a blood patch
Document the findings
Having the patient empty the bladder before the LP
Keeping the patient sitting upright for hours afterward
Positioning side-lying with knees to the chest
Letting the patient move during needle insertion
Labeling CSF tubes in order and sending them
Restricting fluids after the procedure
Having the patient lie flat after the LP
Ignoring numbness and tingling in the legs

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
